topic: Greptile AI Code Review Tool Review (2026)

competitors.title: Greptile Review (2026) - AI Code Review
key_points: 4.5/5 rating, $30/dev/month starting price, no free tier (14-day trial only)
13 languages supported, 8 integrations (GitHub, GitLab, Slack, Jira, Notion, Google Drive, Sentry, VS Code)
82% bug catch rate in independent benchmarks; 100% high-severity bug catch rate (vs 57% Copilot, 36% CodeRabbit)
Multi-hop investigation engine (v3 built on Anthropic Claude Agent SDK) traces dependencies across entire codebase
Full codebase indexing with code graph construction — reviews entire PR in full repository context, not just the diff
Confidence scores on every review comment (43% of v4 comments addressed by developers, up from 30%)
Y Combinator backed, $30M funding, ~20 employees; v4 release in early 2026 improved accuracy and reduced false positives
Higher false positive rate than competitors (11 vs CodeRabbit's 2 in benchmarks); no Bitbucket/Azure DevOps support
Natural language codebase queries — ask questions like 'How does authentication work?' and get accurate answers
Reviews take several minutes (vs 30 seconds for Copilot) due to deeper multi-hop analysis

competitors.title: CodeRabbit vs Greptile (2026): AI Code Review Benchmark Comparison
key_points: CodeRabbit ranks #1 with 51.5% F1, 50.5% precision, 52.5% recall across 317K reviews; Greptile #2 with 50.2% F1, 66.2% precision, 40.4% recall across 53K reviews
Key tradeoff: CodeRabbit catches more issues (higher recall) but generates more noise; Greptile catches fewer but most comments are actionable (higher precision)
Greptile codebase understanding: full code graph + multi-hop investigation vs CodeRabbit's PR diff + incremental context
Greptile has confidence scores per comment, REST API access, self-hosted on AWS/GCP/Azure/air-gapped; CodeRabbit has SOC 2 Type II, .coderabbit.yaml config, broader platform support
Pricing: Greptile $30/dev/month (50 reviews included, $1/additional); CodeRabbit $24/dev/month annual or $30 monthly (unlimited reviews)
Greptile becomes significantly more expensive for teams shipping >50 PRs/dev/month; 50 devs at 100 PRs each = $4,000/month vs CodeRabbit's $1,200/month
Greptile learns from team PR comments (implicit learning); CodeRabbit uses declarative .coderabbit.yaml path-scoped rules
After v4, 43% of Greptile comments get addressed by developers (up from 30%)

competitors.title: AI Code Review Benchmarks 2025 | Greptile
key_points: Official benchmark across 50 real-world bugs from Sentry, Cal.com, Grafana, Keycloak, and Discourse repositories
Overall bug catch rates: Greptile 82%, Cursor Bugbot 58%, GitHub Copilot 54%, CodeRabbit 44%, Graphite 6%
Critical severity: Greptile 58%, Bugbot 58%, Copilot 50%, CodeRabbit 33%, Graphite 17%
High severity: Greptile 100%, Bugbot 64%, Copilot 57%, CodeRabbit 36%, Graphite 0%
Medium+Low severity: Greptile 88%, Bugbot 58%, Copilot 55%, CodeRabbit 55%, Graphite 6%
Benchmark methodology: 5 open-source repos, 10 real bug-fix PRs each, all tools with default settings, line-level comment required to count as caught

competitors.title: Series A and Greptile v3 | Greptile Blog
key_points: $25M Series A led by Benchmark Capital (September 2025), with continued support from YC and Initialized Capital
Greptile reviewed 500M+ lines of code in one month for companies like Brex, Substack, PostHog, Bilt, and YC's internal software team
Greptile helped prevent 180,000+ bugs across its customer base
v3 catches 3x more critical bugs than v2, built on Anthropic Claude Agent SDK for multi-hop investigation
v3 introduced learning from team PR comments (isolated per org), MCP server for IDE/agent integration, Jira/Notion integrations
Auto-detects CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules, and other config files to personalize review feedback

stats.fact: Greptile achieves 82% bug catch rate in independent benchmarks, leading all AI code review tools
Greptile catches 100% of high-severity bugs vs 64% Bugbot, 57% Copilot, 36% CodeRabbit, 0% Graphite
Greptile precision is 66.2% vs CodeRabbit 50.5%, meaning 2 out of 3 Greptile comments flag real issues
CodeRabbit recall is 52.5% vs Greptile 40.4% — CodeRabbit catches ~30% more issues overall
After v4, 43% of Greptile comments are addressed by developers, up from 30% in v3
Greptile reviewed 500M+ lines of code in a single month and helped prevent 180,000+ bugs across customers including Brex, Substack, PostHog
Greptile raised $25M Series A led by Benchmark Capital at $180M valuation, following $4.1M seed from YC and Initialized Capital
Greptile pricing: $30/dev/month (50 reviews included), CodeRabbit: $24/dev/month annual (unlimited reviews), no free tier
Greptile produces 11 false positives vs CodeRabbit's 2 in benchmark testing — tradeoff for aggressive detection
CodeRabbit processed 317,301 reviews vs Greptile's 52,699 in MorphLLM benchmark dataset (6x volume)
AI code assistant market estimated at $6B in 2026 with 22% CAGR, 84% developer adoption
v3 release (September 2025) catches 3x more critical bugs than v2, built on Anthropic Claude Agent SDK

angles: Precision vs Recall tradeoff: Greptile's 66.2% precision means fewer false alarms but misses ~60% of issues; CodeRabbit's 52.5% recall catches more but with more noise — which matters more for your team?

Full-codebase context as differentiator: Greptile indexes your entire repository to build a code graph, catching cross-file dependency breaks and architectural drift that diff-only tools miss entirely
Confidence scores for triage: Greptile's per-comment confidence scoring helps developers prioritize high-certainty findings (43% address rate post-v4) — a UX innovation that most competitors lack
Pricing model implications: Greptile's review-based pricing ($30/seat + $1/review over 50) can cost 3-4x more than CodeRabbit's flat $24/seat for high-volume teams shipping 80-100 PRs/dev/month
Platform support gap: GitHub and GitLab only limits Greptile for Bitbucket/Azure DevOps teams, where CodeRabbit and Qodo are the only viable options
The emerging agentic code review category: Greptile, Cursor Bugbot, and code review agents represent a shift from linting to investigation — understanding why code is wrong, not just that it is
Learning from team behavior: Greptile's implicit learning from engineer PR comments creates org-specific review norms, contrasting with CodeRabbit's explicit .coderabbit.yaml approach

Self-hosted enterprise deployment: Greptile offers air-gapped deployment on AWS/GCP/Azure for compliance-heavy orgs, while CodeRabbit provides SOC 2 Type II certification — different enterprise readiness paths
keywords: greptile vs github copilot code review
outline: Introduction: Why Greptile Matters in 2026 — Full-Codebase AI Code Review That Catches What Others Miss
What Is Greptile? — Y Combinator-backed AI Code Review with Full Repository Understanding
How Greptile Works — Code Graph Construction, Multi-Hop Investigation, and Confidence Scoring
Greptile v3 and v4 — Agent-Based Architecture on Claude Agent SDK, 3x Critical Bug Improvement
Benchmark Results — 82% Bug Catch Rate, 100% High-Severity Detection, and Honest Precision/Recall Tradeoffs
Greptile vs CodeRabbit — Precision (66.2%) vs Recall (52.5%), Platform Support, and Pricing Analysis
Greptile vs GitHub Copilot Code Review — Depth vs Speed, Codebase Context vs Shallow Diff Analysis
Greptile vs Qodo and Cursor Bugbot — Review-Only Depth vs Full Quality Platform vs Editor Ecosystem
Pricing Deep Dive — Per-Seat vs Per-Review Models, Break-Even Analysis, and Enterprise Costs
Key Features — Natural Language Queries, Custom Rules, Jira/Notion Integration, MCP Server, API Access
Strengths — Best-in-Class Bug Detection, Codebase Intelligence, and Developer Trust Metrics
Weaknesses — No Free Tier, Higher False Positives, Platform Limitations, and Slower Reviews
Who Should Use Greptile — Complex Codebases, Security-Critical Teams, and Onboarding Knowledge Bases
Who Should Look Elsewhere — Small Teams, Bitbucket/Azure DevOps Users, and Low-Volume PR Teams
The Bigger Picture — Agentic Code Review and the Future of AI-Assisted Quality Assurance
Conclusion and Recommendation — Greptile for Depth, CodeRabbit for Breadth, and How to Choose
